title: Painting a bathroom: walls and ceiling
subtitle: Apply moisture-resistant paint to a bathroom in the correct sequence
excerpt: How to paint a bathroom without causing mould or peeling: choosing moisture-resistant paint, preparing the surfaces, cutting in around fittings, applying coats in the right order, and allowing adequate ventilation during and after the job.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: An emulsion or masonry paint with a biocide additive that inhibits mould and fungal growth in damp or humid conditions. Required in bathrooms without adequate ventilation, and recommended in all bathrooms as a standard precaution.

Painting a precise line along the edge of a surface where it meets another surface or fitting, using a brush before the roller covers the main area. A clean cut-in line is what separates a professional-looking finish from an amateur one.

content.text: Bathrooms fail paint faster than any other room in the house. The combination of condensation, splashing, and poor ventilation causes ordinary emulsion to peel from the ceiling, grow mould in corners, and discolour at the basin. Choosing a moisture-resistant
and preparing the surface correctly extends the life of the finish significantly. The sequence matters: ceiling first, walls second, woodwork last.

Bathroom-specific emulsion (moisture-resistant, anti-mould), patching filler, a sanding block, masking tape, a 9-inch roller, a 50 mm brush, a 25 mm brush for tight areas, and a dust sheet. If there is existing mould, also add a mould-killing wash or diluted bleach (1 part bleach to 4 parts water) and protective gloves.

If black mould is present, apply diluted bleach solution with a cloth and leave for 10 minutes, then rinse and allow to dry completely. Painting over active mould without treating it first will cause the new paint to fail in the same areas within months. Address the cause of the mould (inadequate ventilation, cold surfaces causing condensation) as well as the symptom; paint alone will not solve a ventilation problem.

Fill any cracks or holes with lightweight filler, allow to dry, and sand flat. Rub down glossy areas with 120-grit sandpaper to provide a key. Wash down the walls with a sugar-soap solution and allow to dry before masking fittings. Do not attempt to paint over a damp surface; run a dehumidifier or heater for a day if the room has been recently used and is not fully dry.

Start at the ceiling. Cut in around the perimeter with the 50 mm brush, then roll the main field with the 9-inch roller. Two coats are standard; allow the first to dry for 2 hours before the second. Bathroom ceilings take more punishment than any other ceiling surface: use a ceiling-specific formulation if the ceiling is prone to heavy condensation.
Cut in around the ceiling line, skirting, and any tiled areas using the 50 mm sash brush. Work the

line carefully around the basin, bath, and shower screen with the 25 mm brush. Once cut-ins are done, roll the main wall area in even strokes. Two coats minimum for full opacity and moisture resistance.

Keep the bathroom well ventilated for 24 hours after the final coat. The paint must cure before it is exposed to steam; running a hot shower in a freshly painted bathroom before the paint has cured will damage the finish. Most bathroom paints are fully cured in 2 to 4 weeks; the surface may feel dry to touch well before this.

attrs.symptom: Paint peeling in patches on the ceiling
attrs.cause: Excessive condensation; inadequate ventilation; or paint applied to a damp surface.
attrs.fix: Strip the peeling areas, treat with anti-mould wash, allow to dry completely, apply a mist coat, then repaint. Improve ventilation before repainting.

attrs.symptom: Mould returning through new paint within weeks
attrs.cause: Active mould was painted over without being fully killed, or the ventilation problem was not addressed.
attrs.fix: Strip back to bare, treat the wall with a purpose-made fungicidal solution, allow to dry for 3 days, then repaint with anti-mould paint and improve the ventilation.
